solutions.solution:
(viii) All dolls are boxes. No baskets are boxes. → No dolls are baskets.

Two very important additional steps:
Step I: Check for any immediate inferences (implication and conversion)
Considering the following. Here, two statements are given, followed by conclusions.
(i) Statements: All books are chairs.
    All chairs are red.
Conclusions: (i) All books are red.
            (ii) Some red objects are books.

Here, we have (the sentences are already aligned) A + A = A. Hence, conclusion should be: All books are red. But if we convert this conclusion, we obtain: Some red objects are books. Hence, both the conclusions given should be taken as true.

(ii) Statements: Some buses are trucks.
     Some trucks are cars.
Conclusions: (i) Some trucks are buses.
            (ii) Some cars are trucks.

Here, we have (the sentences are already aligned) I + I = Is conclusion. But if we convert the first statement 'Some buses are trucks,' we get 'Some trucks are buses'. Similarly, on converting the second statement, we get 'Some cars are trucks'.
Hence, we observe that although there is no conclusion or mediate inference using steps I and II, still on converting the given statements themselves we find that both the given conclusions are true.

(iii) Statements: All buses are trucks.
      All trucks are cars.
Conclusions: (i) Some buses are trucks.
            (ii) Some cars are buses.

Here, we have, A + A = A. Hence, the conclusion should be: "All buses are cars." But this answer choice is not given. But if we convert this statement to get "Some cars are buses" which is the second given conclusion. Also the immediate implication of "All buses are trucks" is "Some buses are trucks". Hence, here again, both the conclusions are correct.
Hence, the above three examples show that while judging the given conclusions, we should not only take the conclusion drawn from A + A (or any) as correct, but the immediate inferences (immediate implications and conversions) of the given statements as well as of the conclusion drawn in table 6 should also be treated as correct inferences.

Step IV: Check for complementary pair
Conclusions: (i) Some pens are men.
            (ii) Some pens are not men.

We can understand that one of these conclusions must be true. That is, either some pens will be men or some pens will not be men. This is a typical case where the choice 'either (i) or (ii) follows' is true. Remember that this step is applied only when without looking at the statements. Such pairs of conclusions are called complementary pairs. In a complementary pair, at least one of the two statements is always true.
Note: Sometimes we make a complementary pair?
Two statements make a complementary pair if
(a) both of them have the same subject and the same predicate
and
(b) they are of I-O type pair or an A-O type pair or an I-E type pair.
Look at the following examples:

Ex. I: (i) All gardens are bulbs.
      (ii) Some gardens are not bulbs.

Pair II: (i) Some gardens are bulbs.
        (ii) Some gardens are not bulbs.

Pair III: (i) Some gardens are bulbs.
         (ii) No gardens are bulbs.

Pair IV: (i) All gardens are bulbs.
        (ii) No gardens are bulbs.

Pair V: (i) All gardens are bulbs.
       (ii) Some bulbs are not gardens.

Comments: In the above example, pairs I, II and III make a complementary pair each while pairs' IV and V don't. In pair I, the first statement is of type A and second is of type O. An A-O pair makes a complementary pair. In pair II, the first statement is of type I and the second is of type O. An I-O pair makes a complementary pair. In pair III, the first statement is of type I while the second is of type E. An I-E pair makes a complementary pair. In pair IV, the first statement is of type A while the second is of type E. An A-E pair does not make a complementary pair. In pair V, we have an A-O pair and an A-O pair does make a complementary pair, yet this pair will not be a complementary pair because the two sentences do not have the same subject and predicate.

Note: Step IV asks you to check for a complementary pair. If you do find a complementary pair you should choose "either of them follows". But remember that step IV is not needed if one of the inferences has already been found to be valid in steps II or III. Step IV is applicable to only those conclusions which do not follow from steps II or III.
